claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 3 children:
  - Alex is 20 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 16 years old and does not live with you.
  - Charlie is 5 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 20, which is 20 or over
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 13 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 16 and in the 20-week extension period
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£0.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Charlie:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 5 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 20, which is 20 or over

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start by gathering some information about your situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Child Benefit eligibility is determined on a per-child basis, so I'll need to know about each child you're inquiring about.

Let me start with the first question:

**Do you have a child (or children) that you're responsible for? If yes, could you tell me their age(s)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do have children. I've got three kids actually. Alex is 20, Blake is 16, and Charlie is 5 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! You have three children - Alex (20), Blake (16), and Charlie (5). I'll need to assess eligibility for each of them separately as the rules differ based on age.

Let me start with your youngest child, Charlie, who is 5 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 5):** Is Charlie working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

(I realize this might seem like an unusual question for a 5-year-old, but I need to go through the standard checks!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Oh goodness, no! Charlie's only 5 - he's not working at all, he's just in primary school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's perfectly understandable! Let me continue with Charlie's assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Charlie (age 5):** Is Charlie receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Charlie doesn't receive any benefits in his own name. He's just a little one.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**For Charlie (age 5):** Is Charlie in any type of apprenticeship?

(Again, I know this seems unusual for a 5-year-old, but these are the standard checks!)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, of course not - he's only 5! He's just in primary school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, thank you. Now I need to ask some questions about **your** circumstances.

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Charlie (age 5):** Do you live with Charlie, or contribute at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards his care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Charlie lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Charlie (age 5):** Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Charlie?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Charlie (age 5):** Is Charlie currently in hospital, in care (looked after by the local council), or in a fostering arrangement? Or is he living normally at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's living normally at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Charlie (age 5):** Is the local council paying towards Charlie's accommodation or maintenance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for Charlie.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! **Charlie (age 5) qualifies for Child Benefit.**

Now let me assess your other two children. Let me start with **Blake, who is 16 years old.**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Blake (age 16):** Is Blake currently in education or training, or has Blake recently left education (within the last 20 weeks)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake recently left education - signed up with the careers service to figure out what to do next.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for that information. Let me check Blake's eligibility for the extension period.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Blake (age 16):** How long ago did Blake leave education? Was it within the last 20 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, it was within the last 20 weeks.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: **For Blake (age 16):** Is Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Blake's actually working full-time now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I see. **Blake (age 16) is not eligible for Child Benefit** because Blake is working 24 or more hours per week and is not in approved education or training.

Now let me assess **Alex, who is 20 years old.**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Alex (age 20) is not eligible for Child Benefit** because Child Benefit is only available for children under 16, or those aged 16-19 who are in approved education or training.

---

## Summary of Eligibility for Your Three Children:

✅ **Charlie (age 5): ELIGIBLE** - Charlie meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

❌ **Blake (age 16): NOT ELIGIBLE** - Blake is working full-time (24+ hours per week) and is not in approved education or training.

❌ **Alex (age 20): NOT ELIGIBLE** - Alex is over the age limit for Child Benefit, which only covers children under 20.

**What this means:** You can claim Child Benefit for Charlie. You should apply online or by post using form CH2. You'll need to provide proof of identity for yourself and Charlie, as well as Charlie's birth certificate.

Please note: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge, though you can still claim the benefit.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about your Child Benefit claim?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Thank you for going through all that with me. That's really helpful to know that I can claim for Charlie. I appreciate you explaining everything so clearly!
